episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Indigenous Lands & Innovation
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [APTN News] The next test for economic reconciliation is explored at Canada 2020 conference and [APTN News] Some people with medical needs are being taken out of Peguis First Nation, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can Indigenous communities deploy their territorial knowledge to build sovereignty rather than create vulnerability? Topics include: Institutional embeddedness vs. co-optation risk in Indigenous-state partnerships, Embodied knowledge transmission through cultural forms as resistance to data extraction, Technology platform ownership and long-term jurisdictional control, Capacity gaps between well-resourced and underfunded Indigenous nations, Digital security and surveillance as extensions of territorial knowledge vulnerabilities.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Denisiqi Services Society

discussion.central_question: How can Indigenous communities deploy their territorial knowledge to build sovereignty rather than create vulnerability?
discussion.riley_position: Indigenous knowledge is most powerful when held and deployed by communities in forms they control toward ends they've defined, with multiple viable strategies (institutional integration, cultural transmission, legal frameworks) available depending on capacity.
riley_key_evidence: 5,200-kilometre Canadian Rangers patrol combining GPS and traditional land knowledge as a geopolitical assertion of Arctic sovereignty
Tla'amin choreographer Cameron sinkʷə Fraser-Monroe's ballet *Cikilaxʷm: Controlled Burn* uses Western art forms to carry Indigenous fire stewardship knowledge to new audiences
Tŝilhqot'in Nation's 2014 William case established Aboriginal title through presentation of territorial knowledge as legal evidence
discussion.casey_position: Indigenous knowledge recognition tends to be conditional on serving state or economic priorities and risks being deprioritized when those priorities shift; communities need structural vigilance around security, jurisdiction, and data ownership from the design stage.
casey_key_evidence: Arctic Rangers program elevated when geopolitical stakes are high but recognition may disappear if pressure eases
BC government's characterization of First Nations consultation on old-growth logging distorts community involvement and narration rights
Historical pattern of Indigenous land knowledge being weaponized through maps, resource inventories, and testimony in extraction processes
discussion.resolution: Both speakers converged on the need for community-controlled infrastructure and ownership structures (like FirstVoices) that remain durable over decades, and agreed that surveillance and security concerns are justified based on historical precedent, not paranoia.
topics_covered: Institutional embeddedness vs. co-optation risk in Indigenous-state partnerships
Embodied knowledge transmission through cultural forms as resistance to data extraction
Technology platform ownership and long-term jurisdictional control
Capacity gaps between well-resourced and underfunded Indigenous nations
Digital security and surveillance as extensions of territorial knowledge vulnerabilities
